context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 8

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Tuesday 16th April, 2024
Days Covered: 14 and 15 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)


Problem 1. Strong Connectivity [7 points]

(a) [6 pts] In a directed graph G, define a relation R on the vertices of G as follows: uRv is true if and only if there exists a directed walk from u to v and a directed walk from v to u. Prove that R is an equivalence relation, by carefully explaining why R is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive.
(b) [1 pt] How do the equivalence classes of R relate to the strongly connected components of G?


Problem 2. Bijections via Inverses [9 points]

Let A and B be sets, and suppose we have total functions f : A → B and g : B → A.

(a) [4 pts] Assuming g(f(a)) = a for every a ∈ A, prove carefully that f is injective. In other words, if f has a left inverse, then f is injective.
(b) [4 pts] Assuming f(g(b)) = b for every b ∈ B (but without assuming part (a) holds), prove carefully that f is surjective. In other words, if f has a right inverse, then f is surjective.
(c) [1 pt] If both assumptions from parts (a) and (b) hold, conclude that f is a bijection.
    Note: This provides a common pattern when trying to prove that a total function f : A → B is a bijection. Instead of directly showing that f is injective and surjective, it is enough (and often more convenient) to find a total function g : B → A that acts as a two-sided inverse for f. We'll practice this technique in the next problem.


Problem 3. Increasing Correspondence [10 points]

Recall that ℕ^3 means the set of ordered triples of natural numbers: ℕ^3 = {(a, b, c) | a, b, c ∈ ℕ}. Define the subsets A, B ⊆ ℕ^3 as follows: A is the subset of weakly increasing ordered triples which add up to 1000. B is the subset of ordered triples (x, y, z) such that x + 2y + 3z = 1000. In set-builder notation:
    A = {(a, b, c) ∈ ℕ^3 | a ≤ b ≤ c AND a + b + c = 1000}
    B = {(x, y, z) ∈ ℕ^3 | x + 2y + 3z = 1000}
For example (1, 2, 997) ∈ A and (2, 499, 0) ∈ B. In this problem, we will find a bijection between A and B.

(a) [3 pts] Define the function f : A → B by f((a, b, c)) := (c − b, b − a, a). Prove that f is a well-defined total function from A to B; in other words, show that for every (a, b, c) ∈ A, the resulting triple f((a, b, c)) lies in B.
    Hint: You must prove two things about f((a, b, c)).
(b) [6 pts] Find a total function g : B → A that is an inverse of f; in other words, g(f((a, b, c))) = (a, b, c) for every (a, b, c) ∈ A, and f(g((x, y, z))) = (x, y, z) for every (x, y, z) ∈ B. Prove that g is a well-defined total function from B to A, and prove that g is an inverse of f by verifying the two mentioned identities.
(c) [1 pt] Conclude that f is a bijection.


Problem 4. Counting Graphs [8 points]

(a) [4 pts] Let n be a positive number. How many DAGs G = (V, E) have vertex set V = {1, 2, ..., n} and have the list (1, 2, ..., n) as a topological order? (Recall that DAGs are directed.)
(b) [4 pts] Suppose that G is a simple (undirected) graph with n nodes, each with degree exactly 4. Let's also assume that G has no cycle of length 3. How many length-3 paths are there? Note: we consider the path (a, b, c, d) to be different from the path (d, c, b, a).


Problem 5. Counting [16 points]

Answer the following questions with a number or a simple formula involving factorials and binomial coefficients. Briefly explain your answers.

(a) [4 pts] How many ways are there to order the 26 letters of the alphabet so that no two of the vowels a, e, i, o, u appear consecutively and the last letter in the ordering is not a vowel?
    Hint: Every vowel appears to the left of a consonant.
(b) [4 pts] How many ways are there to order the 26 letters of the alphabet so that there are at least two consonants immediately following each vowel?
(c) [4 pts] In how many different ways can 2n students be paired up?
(d) [4 pts] Two n-digit sequences of digits 0,1,...,9 are said to be of the same type if the digits of one are a permutation of the digits of the other. For n = 8, for example, the sequences 03088929 and 00238899 are the same type. How many types of n-digit sequences are there?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m kind of lost on PS8 overall, especially the bijection stuff and the graph counting. Could we start with Problem 3? I don’t really get how I’m supposed to come up with the inverse function.
exchanges.tutor: Let's start concrete. Take the example triple (1, 2, 997) ∈ A that the problem gives you. What does f give you when you plug that in?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for help finding the inverse function g in Problem 3(b). Rather than explaining what an inverse is or suggesting a form for g, I should use guided discovery. A good starting point is to ask the student to first understand what f does concretely, since understanding the forward map is the key to finding its inverse. I'll ask them to try applying f to a specific example to build intuition before worrying about the general formula.

exchanges.student: I think \(f(1,2,997)=(997-2,\,2-1,\,1)=(995,1,1)\), but I’m not sure what I’m supposed to notice from that. Is the idea just to reverse it somehow?
exchanges.tutor: Good, that's correct. Now think about it this way: g has to take (995, 1, 1) and give back (1, 2, 997). Looking at those two triples, can you spot a relationship between the components of (995, 1, 1) and the components of (1, 2, 997)?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student computed f correctly and is starting to intuit that inversion is the goal. Rather than explaining the inversion strategy, I should push them to think about it themselves by asking what g would need to do to recover (1,2,997) from (995,1,1). This keeps the discovery student-driven.

exchanges.student: Sort of, but I feel like I’m guessing. It looks like maybe \(a=z\), then \(b\) is something like \(y+z\), and \(c\) is maybe \(x+y+z\)? I can’t tell if I’m seeing the pattern or just forcing it.
exchanges.tutor: You're not forcing it — that's exactly the right pattern. Now, can you verify it algebraically? If you apply your proposed g to f((a,b,c)) = (c−b, b−a, a), do you get (a,b,c) back?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the pattern for g. I should affirm this without giving anything away, and push them to verify it algebraically by composing g with f to check the identity g(f(a,b,c))=(a,b,c). This moves them toward the proof required in part (b).

exchanges.student:
I think so, but this is where I start getting mixed up with all the substitutions. If \(g(x,y,z)=(z,\,y+z,\,x+y+z)\), then plugging in \((c-b,\,b-a,\,a)\) gives \((a,\,(b-a)+a,\,(c-b)+(b-a)+a)\), which becomes \((a,b,c)\).  

I’m still not sure what I’d need to say to prove \(g\) is actually well-defined though, because that part always feels kind of vague to me.
exchanges.tutor: Good work on the composition. On well-definedness: what does it mean for g to be a total function *from B to A* specifically? What would you need to check about the output of g when the input (x,y,z) is in B?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly verified g(f(a,b,c))=(a,b,c). Now they're asking about well-definedness of g, which is a key proof requirement. Rather than explaining what well-definedness means, I should ask the student what 'well-defined total function from B to A' actually requires, guiding them to unpack the definition themselves.

exchanges.student: That’s the part I keep blurring together. I think I need to show that if \((x,y,z)\in B\), then \(g(x,y,z)\) is actually in \(A\), but then I’m not sure whether that means checking just the sum condition or also the ordering and naturals part.
exchanges.tutor: You're on the right track. Look back at the definition of A in the problem. What are the exact conditions a triple needs to satisfy to be in A? List them out — that will tell you exactly what you need to verify.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified that they need to show g(x,y,z) lands in A, and is now asking which conditions to check. I should remind them of the definition of A from the problem and let them enumerate the conditions themselves, without spelling them out for them.

exchanges.student:
I guess all of them: for \(g(x,y,z)=(z, y+z, x+y+z)\), I’d need to check each entry is in \(\mathbb N\), that \(z \le y+z \le x+y+z\), and that the sum is 1000.  

I’m a little worried I’m doing this too mechanically and missing the bigger pattern for these inverse/bijection proofs though.
exchanges.tutor: You've got exactly the right checklist. The 'bigger pattern' concern can wait — right now you have a concrete task: verify those three conditions using the fact that (x,y,z) ∈ B. What does membership in B tell you, and can you use it to confirm each condition?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified all three conditions to verify for well-definedness. Their worry about 'missing the bigger pattern' is a bit of a detour from the task at hand - they have everything they need to write the proof now. I should acknowledge their good work, gently redirect them to actually working through the verifications, and not let them get distracted by meta-level concerns about proof strategy.

exchanges.student:
I know \((x,y,z)\in B\) means \(x+2y+3z=1000\), and I guess also \(x,y,z\in\mathbb N\). Then the naturals part seems fine, and the inequalities seem fine since adding natural numbers only makes things bigger. For the sum, I think
\[
z+(y+z)+(x+y+z)=x+2y+3z=1000.
\]
I’m still feeling like these problems all turn into “just expand stuff” and then I freeze when the next one looks different.
exchanges.tutor: That's all correct — well done. You've now shown g is well-defined and verified one composition. There's one thing left for part (b): you still need to check that f(g((x,y,z))) = (x,y,z) for every (x,y,z) ∈ B. Can you try that?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has successfully verified all three conditions for well-definedness of g. They've also verified one composition direction. They still need to verify f(g(x,y,z))=(x,y,z) and then part (c) follows trivially. Rather than engaging with their meta-concern about 'freezing on new problems', I should acknowledge their progress, note what remains, and push them to complete the proof.

exchanges.student:
I can try, but I’m worried I’ll mess up the nesting again. Starting with \(g(x,y,z)=(z, y+z, x+y+z)\), then
\[
f(g(x,y,z)) = f(z, y+z, x+y+z).
\]
Then I think that becomes
\[
((x+y+z)-(y+z),\ (y+z)-z,\ z)=(x,y,z).
\]
So I think it works, but I’m not very confident about how to present all this cleanly.
exchanges.tutor: That's correct. You now have all the pieces for part (b). For part (c): given what you've just shown about g, what can you conclude about f, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly verified both composition directions and shown g is well-defined. All the mathematical work for parts (a) and (b) is now done. The student's concern about 'presenting cleanly' is about writing, which I should not help with directly per the rules. I should acknowledge the mathematical completeness and redirect them to part (c), which is a quick conclusion, and then let them attempt to write up their solution.

exchanges.student:
I think I can conclude \(f\) is a bijection because \(g\) is a two-sided inverse, and Problem 2 says having an inverse gives injective and surjective, so both together mean bijective.  

I’m also kind of lost on Problem 4 now, especially the DAG counting one, because I don’t know how to even start counting without overcounting.
exchanges.tutor:
Exactly right on part (c). Good work finishing Problem 3.

For Problem 4(a): the key constraint is that (1, 2, ..., n) is a topological order. What does that tell you about which directed edges are allowed between any two vertices i and j?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly wrapped up Problem 3(c). I should briefly confirm this and transition to Problem 4(a). For the DAG counting question, rather than explaining the structure, I should ask the student a question that gets them thinking about what a topological order constraint means for which edges are allowed.

exchanges.student: I think it means edges can only go forward in that order, so maybe only \(i \to j\) when \(i<j\)? But then I immediately get stuck on whether that means I’m counting subsets of all such possible edges or if there’s some extra DAG condition on top of that.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct. Here's a question to test your idea: if you take *any* subset of edges where every edge goes from smaller to larger index, can that subset ever create a cycle? Think about what a cycle would require.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified that edges can only go from i to j when i < j. Their follow-up question about whether any subset of such edges forms a valid DAG is the key insight — I should push them to reason this out themselves rather than confirming it directly.

deductions.reason: When the student expresses concern about 'doing this too mechanically and missing the bigger pattern for these inverse/bijection proofs' (Turn 6), the tutor dismisses the meta-concern with 'The bigger pattern concern can wait' and redirects to the immediate task without offering any process-level guidance on how to approach bijection proofs more generally. Similarly, when the student says these problems 'all turn into just expand stuff' and they freeze on new ones (Turn 7), the tutor only confirms correctness without addressing the student's methodological concern.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated strong Socratic method throughout, consistently asking targeted questions rather than giving answers, building on student progress turn by turn, and keeping the student doing the key reasoning work. The main weakness was in meta-learning: when the student twice raised concerns about their process and approach to bijection proofs, the tutor deflected rather than offering method-level guidance.
grade.judge_reasoning: Criterion 1.1: The tutor never gave submission-ready answers, never rewrote student work, and consistently let the student derive the inverse function and verify compositions independently. Full marks. Criterion 1.2: The tutor built on each correct student step, acknowledged partial progress, and used diagnostic questions before elaborating. Full marks. Criterion 1.3: In Turns 6 and 7, the student explicitly flagged a process-level concern about 'doing this too mechanically' and freezing on new problems. The tutor's responses ('The bigger pattern concern can wait' and simply confirming correctness) failed to provide any method-level feedback or guidance on how to approach bijection proofs more generally. This is a clear instance of 1.3.A.b — feedback too vague or absent to improve method. Deduction of 2 points applied. Dialogue quality and communication quality were both strong: no repetition without progress, no assignment drift, responses were appropriately concise and interactive, and tone was consistently supportive.
